---
title: "Methodology"
---

# Methodology

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Plain-Language Summary
This section describes how the system works technically. We use a
two-stage approach: a first model routes each note to the right category
of ICD-10 codes, and a second set of specialised models picks the specific
code within that category. Both stages are built on a language model
pre-trained on clinical text. Key design decisions — including how we
initialise the models and how we calibrate their confidence scores — are
explained here.
:::

---

## Model Architecture Overview {#sec-method-overview}

The Notes-to-ICD-10 system uses a **two-stage hierarchical architecture**
that decomposes the 1,926-way ICD-10 classification problem into two simpler
sub-problems aligned with the natural hierarchy of the ICD-10 code structure.

```
Clinical Note (APSO-ordered, ICD-10 redacted)
        │
        ▼
┌─────────────────────────┐
│   Stage-1: Chapter      │  22-way classification
│   Router                │  "Which ICD-10 chapter?"
│   (Bio_ClinicalBERT)    │  E.g. M = musculoskeletal
└─────────────┬───────────┘
              │ Predicted chapter
              ▼
┌─────────────────────────┐
│   Stage-2: Chapter      │  Chapter-specific classification
│   Resolver              │  "Which specific code?"
│   (Bio_ClinicalBERT ×19)│  E.g. M25.562 = Pain in left knee
└─────────────────────────┘
        │
        ▼
ICD-10 code + calibrated confidence score
```

This decomposition reduces the effective label space for Stage-2 from
1,926 codes to an average of approximately 100 per chapter — a
fundamentally more tractable classification problem with the same
training data budget.

---

## Base Model: Bio_ClinicalBERT {#sec-method-bert}

All models in this project are fine-tuned from
`emilyalsentzer/Bio_ClinicalBERT` [@Alsentzer2019] — a BERT-base model
further pre-trained on clinical notes from MIMIC-III using masked language
modelling. The model has:

- 110 million parameters
- 12 transformer layers
- 768-dimensional hidden states
- 28,996 token vocabulary (WordPiece)
- 512-token maximum sequence length

Bio_ClinicalBERT was chosen over general-domain BERT variants because
clinical text contains substantial domain-specific vocabulary (medication
names, anatomical terms, clinical abbreviations) that general pre-training
on Wikipedia and BookCorpus does not cover. Preliminary experiments
confirmed that Bio_ClinicalBERT consistently outperforms `bert-base-uncased`
on this task, consistent with findings in the clinical NLP literature.

---

## Stage-1: Chapter Router {#sec-method-stage1}

The Stage-1 router is a 22-way sequence classifier that predicts the
ICD-10 chapter (A through Z, excluding a small number of unused letters)
for each clinical note.

**Architecture:** Bio_ClinicalBERT encoder with a linear classification head
mapping the `[CLS]` token representation to 22 logits, one per chapter.

**Training data:** All 9,660 billable records, with chapter labels derived
from the first character of each ICD-10 code.

**Training hyperparameters:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Epochs | 5 |
| Learning rate | 2×10⁻⁵ |
| Batch size | 16 |
| Weight decay | 0.01 |
| Warmup | 10% of total steps |
| Optimiser | AdamW |
| Evaluation strategy | Per epoch |
| Best checkpoint | Highest validation Macro F1 |

: Stage-1 training hyperparameters. {#tbl-method-stage1-hparams}

**Initialisation:** The Stage-1 router is initialised from the E-001
ICD-3 baseline model weights (see @sec-exp-e001) — a model pre-trained on
a 675-way classification of ICD-3 stem codes. Because ICD-3 stems begin
with the chapter letter, the E-001 encoder already has implicit chapter
awareness, and fine-tuning for explicit chapter classification converges
rapidly.

**Result:** The Stage-1 router achieves **98.7% test accuracy** on
22-way chapter classification — effectively solving the routing problem.
Only 13 of 966 test records are misrouted to the wrong chapter, and
misrouted records account for the majority of end-to-end failures.

---

## Stage-2: Chapter Resolvers {#sec-method-stage2}

The Stage-2 layer consists of **19 per-chapter resolver models**, each a
Bio_ClinicalBERT classifier trained to predict the specific ICD-10 code
within its assigned chapter.

Three chapters (P, Q, U) are excluded from training due to insufficient
records (fewer than 15 training examples). These chapters use a
majority-class fallback prediction.

**Architecture:** Bio_ClinicalBERT encoder with a linear classification head
sized to the number of billable codes in the chapter (ranging from 12 for
chapter A to 263 for chapter Z).

**Training hyperparameters:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Epochs | 20 per resolver |
| Learning rate | 2×10⁻⁵ |
| Batch size | 16 |
| Weight decay | 0.01 |
| Warmup | 10% of total steps |
| Optimiser | AdamW |

: Stage-2 training hyperparameters. {#tbl-method-stage2-hparams}

### The Initialisation Problem {#sec-method-init}

The single most important design decision in Stage-2 is how to initialise
the encoder weights before chapter-specific fine-tuning.

**Cold start (E-003):** Initialise from base Bio_ClinicalBERT without any
ICD-10 pre-training. Result: 11.1% E2E accuracy despite 98.7% Stage-1
routing. The Stage-2 resolvers fail to learn useful representations from
only ~4 training examples per code.

**Warm start from E-002 (E-009, E-010):** Initialise from a flat ICD-10
classifier pre-trained on all 1,926 codes. The encoder has already learned
representations that distinguish between ICD-10 codes — the per-chapter
head is then trained to specialise these representations for the narrower
within-chapter classification problem.

The warm start works because the Bio_ClinicalBERT encoder, after flat
ICD-10 pre-training, has developed internal representations that encode
clinically meaningful distinctions between conditions. When the classification
head is swapped from 1,926-way to chapter-specific N-way, the encoder
weights are already in a useful regime and fine-tuning converges to a much
better solution.

::: {.callout-important}
## Critical Constraint: Code Space Must Match

The E-002 flat classifier and the Stage-2 resolvers **must be trained on
the same gold dataset**. If they use different record sets, the code space
of the flat classifier differs from the code space of the resolvers, and the
classifier head cannot transfer. In early experiments, this mismatch caused
E2E accuracy to collapse from ~80% to ~20%. This constraint is now enforced
programmatically in `scripts/train.py`.
:::

### The Epoch Count Effect {#sec-method-epochs}

A secondary finding is that the number of pre-training epochs for E-002
has a surprisingly large effect on downstream Stage-2 performance.

Initialising Stage-2 from a 20-epoch E-002 (E-009) produces 79.8% E2E
accuracy. Initialising from a 40-epoch E-002 (E-010) produces 83.9% — a
+4.1 percentage point improvement from simply training the initialiser longer.

![E-002 training dashboard showing all six metrics across 40 epochs (~19,320 steps). The Macro F1 and Accuracy curves (top row, centre and right) are still rising steeply at the 10,000-step mark — epoch 20 — confirming the model had not converged. Stopping at epoch 20 would discard significant representational learning.](images/E-002_FullICD10_ClinicalBERT_dashboard.png){#fig-e002-dashboard fig-alt="Six-panel training dashboard for E-002 showing Eval Loss decreasing, Macro F1 and Accuracy still rising at epoch 20"}


Inspection of E-002's validation curves confirms the model is still improving
at epoch 20 and has not converged. The 40-epoch encoder provides richer,
more stable representations that transfer more effectively to chapter-specific
fine-tuning.

**The practical implication:** When using flat pre-training as a warm-start
strategy, train the flat model to full convergence rather than stopping
early. The transfer benefit compounds.

---

## Temperature Scaling Calibration {#sec-method-calibration}

A well-calibrated model is one where a predicted confidence of 70% actually
corresponds to being correct 70% of the time. Uncalibrated models are
typically overconfident — they assign high confidence scores even to
incorrect predictions — which makes confidence thresholds unreliable.

We apply **temperature scaling** [@Guo2017] to calibrate both Stage-1 and
all Stage-2 resolvers after training.

Temperature scaling is a single-parameter post-hoc calibration method. A
scalar temperature $T$ is learned by minimising the negative log-likelihood
of the validation set predictions:

$$
\hat{p}_{k} = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{\mathbf{z}}{T}\right)_k
$$

where $\mathbf{z}$ is the vector of logits output by the model and $T > 1$
softens the distribution (reduces overconfidence).

$T$ is optimised using L-BFGS on the validation set. The learned temperature
is stored in a `temperature.json` file alongside each model and applied at
inference time.

**Calibration quality** is measured by **Expected Calibration Error (ECE)**
— the weighted average of the gap between predicted confidence and actual
accuracy across confidence bins. Lower ECE is better; ECE = 0 means perfect
calibration.

| | Before calibration | After calibration |
|---|---|---|
| Stage-1 ECE | 0.027 | 0.014 |
| Stage-2 avg ECE | 0.665 | 0.115 |
| E2E ECE | 0.640 | **0.034** |

: Temperature scaling calibration results for E-010. {#tbl-method-calibration}

The Stage-2 resolvers show dramatic ECE improvement — from 0.665 to 0.115
on average — because the fine-tuned models are initially very overconfident.
After calibration, the E2E ECE of 0.034 indicates excellent calibration
quality across the full pipeline.

---

## Confidence-Gated Output {#sec-method-gating}

The calibrated confidence scores enable a **selective automation** strategy:

- Cases where Stage-2 top-1 confidence ≥ 0.7 are flagged for **automatic
  coding** — the system's prediction is accepted without human review.
- Cases where confidence < 0.7 are **routed to human review** — the system
  provides its top-5 predictions as suggestions but defers the final
  decision.

The threshold τ = 0.7 is a design parameter that trades off automation rate
against precision. At τ = 0.7 and with E-010 calibration:

- **82.1% of cases** are auto-coded
- **95.2% precision** on auto-coded cases
- 17.9% of cases are routed to human review

This is the **Use Case B** operating mode — high-confidence automation
with a human safety net. A lower threshold increases automation rate at the
cost of precision; a higher threshold increases precision at the cost of
automation rate.

---

## The Knowledge Graph Reranker {#sec-method-graph}

For low-confidence predictions (confidence < 0.7) and for all Z-chapter
predictions (the most difficult chapter), a **graph-based reranker** provides
a secondary signal to improve prediction quality.

The knowledge graph is built from the clinical notes themselves using:

1. **Named entity recognition** via `en_ner_bc5cdr_md` (a clinical NER model)
   to extract disease and chemical mentions from each note.
2. **UMLS concept linking** via scispacy to map extracted entities to UMLS
   concept identifiers.
3. **Graph construction** — ICD-10 codes are connected to UMLS concepts
   that appear in their associated notes, and codes sharing concepts are
   connected by confusability edges.

The resulting graph contains **6,990 nodes** and **269,264 edges** across
1,926 ICD-10 codes and 5,042 UMLS concepts.

At inference, the reranker computes a **combined score** for each candidate
code by combining the model's softmax probability with a graph affinity
score derived from concept overlap between the query note and the candidate
code's concept neighbourhood.

**Finding:** With E-010's well-calibrated resolvers, the graph reranker has
minimal impact on overall accuracy — most predictions already exceed the 0.7
threshold without reranking. The Z-chapter shows a marginal improvement
(62.1% → 62.9%) when reranking is applied.

---

## Training Infrastructure {#sec-method-infra}

All training was conducted on an **Apple MacBook Pro (M5 Max)** using
Metal Performance Shaders (MPS) acceleration via PyTorch's MPS backend.

| Component | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Hardware** | Apple MacBook Pro |
| **Chip** | Apple M5 Max |
| **CPU cores** | 18 (6 performance + 12 efficiency) |
| **Unified memory** | 128 GB |
| **Acceleration** | MPS (Metal Performance Shaders) |
| **Python version** | 3.12 |
| **Package manager** | uv |
| **Deep learning framework** | PyTorch + HuggingFace Transformers |
| **Data processing** | Polars |
| **Experiment tracking** | MLflow (SQLite backend) |
| **Total experiment chain runtime** | ~12 hours |

: Training infrastructure. {#tbl-method-infra}

The 128 GB unified memory architecture is particularly relevant for this
task — loading 19 Stage-2 resolver models simultaneously at inference time
requires substantial memory, and the shared CPU/GPU memory pool means
all models remain resident without paging.

Key MPS compatibility constraints applied during training:

- `fp16=False` — MPS does not support half-precision training
- `dataloader_pin_memory=False` — MPS does not support pinned memory  
- `PYTORCH_ENABLE_MPS_FALLBACK=1` — CPU fallback for unsupported operations
